content.problemStatement:
Let us begin this week on 'Bin packing' problems with a practice problem

Problem statementChef has NNN empty boxes. There are MMM different colours numbered from 111 to MMM such that Chef has AiA_iAi​ balls of the ithi^{th}ith colour.

A distribution of these balls into boxes is said to be valid if:


Each ball is placed in a box;
All the balls in a box are of different colour.
Over all valid distributions, find the **minimum** number of boxes having MMM balls.

Under the given constraints, it is guaranteed that at least one valid distribution always exists.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the number of empty boxes and the number of colours, respectively.
The next line contains MMM space-separated integers, ithi^{th}ith of which denotes AiA_iAi​ i.e. the number of balls of colour iii.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the minimum number of boxes with MMM balls in a valid distribution.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and M – the number of boxes and the number of colours. The next line contains M space‑separated integers A1 … AM, where Ai is the number of balls of colour i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum possible number of boxes that contain exactly M balls.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Total balls S = 5+4+4 = 13. N·(M‑1) = 5·2 = 10. S‑N·(M‑1) = 3, so at least 3 boxes must be full. The answer is 3.
S = 2, N·(M‑1) = 2·1 = 2, S‑N·(M‑1) = 0. No box needs to be completely filled.

solution.approach: Let S = Σ Ai be the total number of balls. A box can hold at most one ball of each colour, therefore at most M balls. If we have k boxes that are completely filled (contain M balls), the remaining N‑k boxes can hold at most M‑1 balls each. Hence the total capacity with k full boxes is k·M + (N‑k)·(M‑1) = N·(M‑1) + k. A distribution is feasible iff S ≤ N·(M‑1) + k. The smallest integer k satisfying this inequality is k = max(0, S – N·(M‑1)). This value is also ≤ N because S ≤ N·M. The algorithm simply computes S and applies the formula.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M) per test case to sum the Ai values.
